mnemonics.title: IP Classes Made Easy
mnemonics.content:
A: A lot of people eat (8 network bits, large networks)
B: But cannot (16 network bits)
C: Cook dinner (24 network bits, small networks)
D: Directly (Multicast)
E: Eventually (Experimental)

mnemonics.title: Subnet Mask Quick Reference
mnemonics.content:
255 = All bits network (11111111)
0 = All bits host (00000000)
Count the 255s to know the /prefix!

mnemonics.title: Routing Protocol Classification
mnemonics.content:
RIP: Rides In cars (Distance Vector - hop count)
OSPF: Over Speedy Paths Fast (Link State - cost)
BGP: Between Great Provinces (Path Vector - AS paths)

mnemonics.title: Bellman-Ford Steps
mnemonics.content:
Initialize: Set distance to source = 0, others = ∞
Relax: Update distances through each edge
Repeat: For |V|-1 times
Check: Look for negative cycles

acronyms.usage: IP addressing method using variable-length subnet masks to allocate IP addresses efficiently
Maps private IP addresses to a single public IP address
Allows different subnet masks for different subnets within the same network
Link-state interior gateway protocol using Dijkstra's algorithm
Exterior gateway protocol for internet-wide routing between autonomous systems
Distance vector protocol using hop count metric with max 15 hops
Maps IP addresses to MAC addresses on local network
Used in routers to filter traffic based on IP addresses and other criteria

visualTips.visualization: Think of IP address as: [Network bits][Host bits]. Subnet mask shows where the division is - 1s = network, 0s = host. Example: /24 = 255.255.255.0 means first 24 bits are for network, last 8 for hosts.

Use a 3-box diagram: Network ID | Subnet ID | Host ID. Show how borrowing bits from host portion creates subnets. Example: /26 on Class C = 2 bits for subnet, 6 for hosts = 4 subnets with 62 hosts each.
Draw table with columns: Destination | Subnet Mask | Next Hop | Interface. Show how router matches destination IP against entries, finding longest prefix match.
Draw nodes (routers) and edges (links with costs). Start from source, highlight visited nodes. Show 'tentative' and 'permanent' sets. Always pick lowest cost path first.
Show two routers exchanging their routing tables. Each router adds cost to neighbor's routes and updates if shorter path found. Illustrate 'routing by rumor' concept.
Use number line: 10.x.x.x (Class A - large), 172.16-31.x.x (Class B - medium), 192.168.x.x (Class C - small). All are non-routable on internet.

analogies.analogy: Like a postal address: Country (Network) + City (Subnet) + House number (Host). The postal system (routing) delivers mail (packets) based on this hierarchy.
Like a map in a delivery truck. It shows different destinations and which road (next hop) to take. Drivers (routers) consult this map to decide the route.
Like dividing a large apartment building (Class C network) into floors (subnets). Each floor has its own mailroom (router) to manage deliveries within that floor.
Like a building reception desk. All residents (private IPs) give their mail to the receptionist (NAT device) who sends it out using just one building address (public IP).
Like asking neighbors for directions to a destination. Each neighbor tells you how many 'turns' (hops) it takes from their location. You pick the path with fewest turns.
Like each person in a city has a complete map of all roads and their conditions. Everyone shares this map with everyone else, so all have full knowledge of the entire city.
Like an air traffic control tower at an airport. It directs planes (packets) to their appropriate gates (destinations) based on their flight plans (IP addresses).
